retrieved_docs.title: Zirconia Biomaterials -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The restorations are processed either by soft machining of pre-sintered blanks or by hard machining of fully sintered blocks. The mechanical properties of 3Y-TZP are strongly affected by its grain size. The mechanical properties of zirconia have been reported to be higher than other ceramics for dental applications. Fracture resistance of 6–10 MPa/m1/2, a flexural strength of 900–1200 MPa, and a compression resistance of 2000 MPa have been reported. Zirconia restorations bear an average load of 755 N. Zirconia yields higher fracture loads than alumina or lithium disilicate. Zirconia ceramics yield superior wear behavior and lower antagonistic wear compared to porcelain.

retrieved_docs.title: Teeth Polishing -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Extrinsic stains are 1 of the factors that influence tooth color assessment. Performing professional dental prophylaxis before composite or ceramic color selection in anterior teeth restorations could increase treatment predictability. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Zirconia Biomaterials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: One of the technical complications reported in the YTZ-P reconstructions is chipping or delamination of the veneering porcelain. The core-veneer interface is a critical factor in the success of layered restorations. The contributing factors for the fracture may include weakness of the veneer material or the core/veneer bond, stresses and distortion due to the veneering process, unsupported veneering porcelain and configuration of the core and veneer, residual stresses arising from a coefficient of thermal expansion mismatch, and rapid cooling rates after heat treatment. The use of high-speed polishing rotary instruments is contraindicated for clinical use because of nickel contamination, phase changes in zirconia, and the resultant surface roughness.

Mode of supply: Available as light, medium, and heavy body consistencies. 2. Composition: The base contains polyether, fillers, and plasticizers. The accelerator contains alkyl aromatic sulfonates, inert oils, and plasticizers. 3. Setting reaction: When base and accelerator are mixed in equal proportions, polyether rubber is formed. 4. Indications Fixed prosthodontics (crown and bridgework, particularly for multiple preparations) Implants Border molding in complete denture fabrication Indirect inlay and onlay impressions It can be used for corrective impressions as it can be added to previously set material. 5. retrieved_docs.title: Zirconia Biomaterials -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The production process of these blocks consists of compacting zirconia powder in the presence of a binder through a cold, isostatic pressing process; this leads to the homogeneous distribution of the components inside the block. The pre-sintering temperature of zirconia can influence the hardness, machinability, and roughness of the blocks.

retrieved_docs.title: Zirconia Biomaterials -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The increased opacity of zirconia ceramics could be useful in esthetically demanding clinical situations, such as in cases of masking dichromatic abutment teeth or metal post and cores, but low light transmission may cause inadequate polymerization of resin cement under zirconia ceramics. When comparing different ceramic restorative materials, zirconia ceramics have the highest relative translucency compared to metals. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Zirconia Biomaterials -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The tetragonal phase can be retained at room temperature by the addition of 3 mol% yttria. Yttria-stabilized tetragonal zirconia polycrystal (Y-TZP) provides mechanical properties superior to other all-ceramic systems due to the tetragonal-to-monoclinic phase transformation. Thus, Y-TZP has been increasingly used as a core material for all-ceramic restorations. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Zirconia Biomaterials -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Dimensional changes from the sintering process of Y-TZP can lead to non-uniform cement space in single copings. The manufacturing process of Y-TZP blanks may influence the sintering shrinkage pattern and, consequently, on the prostheses fit.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: 2. Composition: All the consistencies are supplied as base and accelerator. The base contains hydroxy-terminated polysiloxane polymer which undergoes crosslinking, and fillers to control viscosity. The accelerator contains divinylpolysiloxane prepolymers, which are crosslinking agents, chloroplatinic acid, which is a catalyst, palladium, which is a hydrogen absorber, and fillers

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Zinc oxide eugenol (ZOE) impression material is an irreversible nonelastic material used as a final impression (wash impression) in complete denture fabrication. It records the tissues in the undistorted state. [9] [10] 1. Composition: ZOE impression material is available in 2 collapsible tubes; 2 contain the base and the other the catalyst (see Image . Zinc Oxide Eugenol). The base contains ZnO 87%, vegetable or mineral oils 13%. The catalyst contains eugenol 12-15%, rosin 50%, fillers 20%, resinous balsam 10%, accelerator, and color pigments 5%. *Noneugenol pastes (for patients who are allergic to eugenol) contain carboxylic acids. 2. Instruments and armamentarium: paper pad or glass slab and broad-end spatula. 3. Manipulation: equal lengths are squeezed out from both tubes on a paper pad or a glass slab and mixed with a broad-end spatula until they are mixed to an even color.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The mechanical properties of hydroxyapatite are influenced by factors such as phase composition, crystal size, and synthesis method. Pure hydroxyapatite typically has a bending strength of 38 to 250 MPa, compressive strength of 120 to 150 MPa, and tensile strength of 38 to 300 MPa. The Young modulus ranges from 35 to 120 GPa, depending on the presence of impurities. [19] Meanwhile, the Weibull modulus, ranging from 5 to 18, indicates hydroxyapatite is a brittle material. To enhance its mechanical properties, tough hydroxyapatite can be obtained by incorporating TCP, which increases its flexural strength. In contrast, the flexural strength reaches its minimum when hydroxyapatite contains calcium oxide CaO. [10] [19] Furthermore, the sintering temperature also significantly alters the mechanical properties of hydroxyapatite. Higher sintering temperatures lead to increased density, compressive strength, grain size, and torsional strength. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrophilic Accurate and high-dimensional stability enables the delay of casting and allows for multiple pourings. Good elastic recovery Good compatibility with gypsum Good shelf life It can be used as a single-phase material or with a syringe tray technique 6. Disadvantage: The most rigid of all the elastomers makes it hard to remove (newer polyethers are slightly more flexible) May cause allergy due to sulfonate acid esters The impression should not be immersed in disinfectant for more than 10 minutes since it's hydrophilic. [22] [23] Since it absorbs water, it should not be kept with alginate impressions. Polysulfide Polysulfides are the first elastomers to be introduced. Although messy to manipulate, it is useful when a long working time is needed. Polysulfides are not recommended except for complete dentures. 1. Consistencies: light or heavy body 2. Mode of supply: It is available in a 2-paste system: base and accelerator.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: 5. Setting reaction: The polysulfide prepolymer reacts with lead oxide, forming polysulfide rubber and water. The reaction byproduct significantly affects the dimensional stability of the impression; therefore, the impression should be cast immediately. Polysulfide is the most flexible of elastomeric materials, and it can be easily removed from undercut areas. [24] 6. Advantages: Least rigid (most flexible of all the elastomers) Good tear strength Most biocompatible Excellent flow, so good reproduction of details Hydrophilic 7. Disadvantages: Requires a custom tray Long setting time Must be poured within half an hour Hydrophobic Bad odor Unpleasant taste Stains the clothes Messy to work 8. Indications: Only recommended in complete denture cases. Condensation Silicone Condensation silicone is more affordable than other elastomeric materials but has the disadvantage of being prone to shrinkage, requiring immediate pouring. 1. Consistencies: extra-low, Low, medium, and putty

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Impression Materials -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The wettability of the impression material determines its ability to record fine details, which depends on its viscosity. The more the impression material wets the tissues, the more it comes in close contact with them and captures fine details. The more viscous materials have limited flow and very few wetting features. [4] In fixed dental prostheses, an impression is expected to reproduce 20 to 70 microns, and 100 to 150 microns in removable prosthodontics. According to international standards, the impression material should record a line of 0.02 mm width or less, which is less than the width of a human hair. [5] Impression Materials Classification: 1. Based on use: For primary impressions, eg, alginate, impression compound, and elastomeric putty. For secondary impressions, eg, light body elastomers and zinc oxide eugenol. 2. Based on the setting reaction:

retrieved_docs.title: Hydroxyapatite Dental Material -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The applications of hydroxyapatite in hard tissue restoration and drug delivery do not typically involve its pure form. Pure hydroxyapatite has relatively low mechanical properties and is brittle, making it unsuitable for load-bearing applications. Consequently, hydroxyapatite is often incorporated into composites or polymers to enhance its functionality. [11] [16] In this case, the enhanced properties of hydroxyapatite result from the compressive strength of the hydroxyapatite ceramic phase, combined with the toughness and elasticity of the polymer or composite matrix. Generally, hydroxyapatite exhibits resistance to resorption in vivo at a rate of 1% to 2% per year, providing long-lasting structural support in the defect area. [8] [12]

Collectively, these relationships illustrate the versatility of calcium carbonate across pharmaceutical categories: it functions as a filler, a calcium source, a buffering or antacid agent, and a structural constituent in both synthetic and natural products, highlighting its importance in formulation science and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 907
retrieved_docs.content: process without contributing therapeutic activity. Calcium carbonate is also present as an inactive ingredient in the oral rinse “sodium fluoride 0.5 MG/ML Mouthwash”, where it likely serves as a buffering agent. The supplement “Multi‑Day with Calcium and Extra Iron” is described as having a narrower relationship to calcium carbonate, indicating that calcium carbonate is a key, though not exclusive, component of its formulation. Finally, the material “perleť” (mother‑of‑pearl) is linked to calcium carbonate through a “has child” relationship, reflecting the natural derivation of nacre from calcium carbonate crystals. Collectively, these relationships illustrate calcium carbonate’s versatile role in human and veterinary medicines—as a therapeutic calcium source, a pH‑modulating agent, a filler or excipient, and even as a structural component of natural biomaterials—underscoring its importance across diverse therapeutic contexts and product types.

Recognising these relationships aids clinicians, formulators and patients in understanding ingredient composition, potential drug‑nutrient interactions, and the rationale behind excipient selection in patient‑focused medication design.

Together, these relationships illustrate that ammonium chloride functions both as a key active electrolyte component in multi‑herbal oral liquid preparations and as a member of a broader electrolyte/mineral replacement group that includes related compounds such as chromic chloride, while also co‑existing with inactive constituents like selenium sulfide in certain formulations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1918
retrieved_docs.content: Replacement preparation is a medical formulation that incorporates several specific components, including kalcium‑glycerolfosfát, dibasic potassium phosphate, and sodium hyaluronate. Kalcium‑glycerolfosfát contributes calcium and phosphate ions that can support mineral balance, while dibasic potassium phosphate provides additional phosphate and potassium ions to help maintain physiological pH and electrolyte homeostasis. Sodium hyaluronate, a high‑molecular‑weight glycosaminoglycan, offers viscoelastic properties that are valuable for joint lubrication and tissue repair. Together, these ingredients define the composition of the replacement preparation, highlighting its role in delivering essential minerals and lubricating agents for therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1831

retrieved_docs.content: 0.0088 MG/MG Toothpaste. Across these preparations, sodium monofluorophosphate provides fluoride ions that inhibit demineralization and promote remineralization of tooth enamel, thereby reducing the incidence of dental caries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1827
retrieved_docs.content: Replacement preparation is a therapeutic formulation that incorporates kalcium-glycerolfosfát and sodium hyaluronate as its constituent components. Kalcium-glycerolfosfát provides a calcium‑phosphate source that can support mineralization processes, while sodium hyaluronate contributes viscoelastic and lubricating properties useful for tissue repair and joint function. By combining these two agents, the replacement preparation is designed to enhance clinical outcomes in procedures that require both structural support and improved biomechanical performance.

Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians and formulators in optimizing patient care, ensuring appropriate dosing, and recognizing the broader therapeutic and supportive contributions of vitamin A within diverse health‑related products.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2353
